title: Chapter 10: The Cheat Code
content: Two AM and my apartment wall is a crime scene.

Three columns of sticky notes — grey, gold, green — stretch from the bookshelf to the window frame, a timeline triptych glowing in the blue light of my laptop. The grey column is Timeline A: meetings, deadlines, Julian A's impossible standards rendered in Sharpie shorthand. Q2 projections. Rebalancing review. Cramer call Thursday. The gold column is Timeline B: gallery invitations in flowing script, Julian B's quiet attention abbreviated to JB —, French verb conjugations I'm apparently learning in my sleep. *Je suis, tu es, il est.* The green column is Timeline C: Julian C's wound healing, the stitches due out Tuesday, Harker approaching like weather on a doppler, the rain that won't stop falling in that particular Paris.
I've been shifting daily now. Sometimes twice.

The espresso on my nightstand has gone cold and bitter, skin forming on the surface like something trying to close over a wound. I drink it anyway. The taste grounds me — burnt, acrid, real in the way only terrible decisions at 2 AM can be. My teeth feel fuzzy. My tongue tastes like copper. I touch my nose reflexively; it comes away clean, but the inside of my left nostril is that specific kind of raw that means the blood was here recently and will be again.
The Fork app sits open on my phone, face-up on the mattress. INTEGRITY: 83%. DECREASING.
I flip the phone over. The screen against the sheet throws a pale square of light toward the ceiling like a window I can't climb through.
I'm too busy winning to read the warnings.
The cheat comes to me on a Tuesday, and it's so simple I laugh out loud.

Sterling has a major client meeting — the Adlar Group, repositioning their emerging market exposure. The meeting's at 10 AM Eastern. Paris is six hours ahead. Which means by the time I sit down in that conference room at 9:45, Timeline B's European markets have been open for almost four hours. The ECB commentary, the bund yield movement, whatever surprise press conference some finance minister is giving — it's already happened over there. It hasn't happened here yet.

I shift before the meeting. Just for three minutes. Long enough to stand in Julian B's apartment while he's still asleep, the Paris dawn greying the curtains, and scroll the Financial Times Europe edition on his kitchen tablet. The lead story: Austrian banking regulator flagging liquidity concerns in a regional lender with significant Eastern European exposure. Not headline news yet in New York. Won't be for hours.
I shift back. The world folds and unfolds. The bathroom of Sterling's thirty-fourth floor tastes like ozone and ambition.

At 10 AM, when Adlar's team mentions their Eastern European positions, I ask about Austrian regional bank exposure. Specific. Targeted. Like I've been reading their risk disclosures all morning instead of skimming them on the elevator.
Silence around the table. The good kind.

Julian A looks at me from the head of the conference table. His pen has stopped moving. His eyes are — there's no other word for it — bright. Not the cold brightness of evaluation. Something warmer. Something that makes my stomach do a thing it has no business doing in a professional setting.
"How did you know about the Austrian situation?" Adlar's CIO asks.
"I read broadly," I say, and it's not even a lie.
After that, it's like I've found a cheat code. Because I have.

Before every major meeting, I shift to Timeline B. A five-minute dip, no more. Check the European news cycle, the Asian close, any early indicator that won't hit New York for another three to six hours. Shift back. Walk into the room with information no one else has. Currency moves, commodity shocks, geopolitical rumblings — I anticipate them all. I'm not psychic. I'm just on a different clock.

Julian A has stopped questioning my preparation. He's stopped with the clipped "Walk me through your methodology" and the suspicious lateral glances across the trading floor. Now when I present, he leans back in his chair and listens. Genuinely listens, the way you listen to someone whose competence has become reliable enough to trust.
Respected. I am respected by Julian Vance, and the feeling is a drug with no comedown.

But it's not just respect. I catch it in fragments — the way his gaze lingers a beat too long when I'm presenting, how he turns his body toward me in meetings even when someone else is speaking. Tuesday, he hands me a file and our fingers touch, paper sliding between them, and he doesn't pull away fast. Neither do I. The static charge of it is probably the air conditioning. It's definitely the air conditioning.
By Thursday, I've nailed three meetings, flagged two risks before they materialized, and produced a sector analysis so prescient that Marcus from Quant actually asked if I had a crystal ball.
"Just good sources," I said, and the espresso in my hand tasted like victory.
The cost arrives on Thursday too. It just arrives differently.

At 12:47 PM, I surface back into my body at my desk and realize I've been gone for forty-seven minutes. My fingers are still on the keyboard — same position, same document on screen. But the coffee next to my elbow has gone from hot to room temperature, and there's a strand of drool at the corner of my mouth I wipe away fast.

Forty-seven minutes. My body was here, staring at nothing, while my consciousness was picking flowers at the Marché aux Fleurs on the Île de la Cité. I can still smell the hyacinths, the wet stone, the particular green sweetness of stems snapped fresh. I can still feel the weight of the bouquet in my hand, the paper wrapper going soft from the dew.
"Avery?"
Danielle from Compliance is standing beside my desk, head tilted. "You okay? You've been... out."
"Low blood sugar," I say. The lie comes smooth as silk. "I need to eat more regularly. Thanks for checking."
She nods, not convinced but not paid enough to push. Walks away.
My hand is trembling. I press it flat against the desk until it stops.

That night, I wake up at 3 AM with the taste of pennies flooding my mouth. The pillowcase under my face is streaked red, a Rorschach of my own making. I stumble to the bathroom, pinch the bridge of my nose, tilt my head forward like they teach you, and watch the blood drip-drip-drip into the white porcelain sink. It takes eleven minutes to stop.

Eleven minutes I spend looking at my own face in the mirror, at the grey shadows under my eyes that concealer can't fully hide anymore, at the raw inside of my nostrils, at the way my collarbones have started to sharpen against the neckline of my t-shirt.

I'm drinking coffee like it's an IV line — six, seven cups a day, the bitterness the only thing that cuts through the fog. I'm sleeping four hours a night across all three timelines combined. Not four each. Four total. My body can't tell which timeline the rest comes from, and neither can I. I close my eyes in Timeline A and dream of Paris rain. I close my eyes in Timeline B and hear Julian C's breathing, steady and wounded. I close my eyes in Timeline C and wake up at my desk with drool on my chin.
The Fork app says INTEGRITY: 81%.
I close the app. I have a meeting in six hours. I need to check the overnight Asian session.
Mika calls on Friday evening.

I'm at my apartment, standing in front of the wall of sticky notes, adding a gold one — *JB: mentioned a Cézanne exhibit, private viewing Thursday* — when my phone lights up with her name. The ringtone is the one I set in college, something by Fiona Apple that used to make us both laugh. It doesn't make me laugh now. It makes me feel like I'm being summoned to the principal's office by someone who loves me.
I answer.
"Hey."

"Don't 'hey' me." Mika's voice is warm but edged, the particular frequency of someone who's been worried and is now irritated at themselves for waiting this long to call. "I haven't seen you in two weeks, Avery. Are you alive?"
"I'm alive." I'm peeling the backing off the sticky note, pressing it to the wall. The adhesive is weak; it curls at the corner. "I'm fine. I'm busy. I'm —"
"Killing it at work, let me guess."
"How did you —"

"Because that's what you always say when you're disappearing. You're 'killing it.' You're 'in the zone.' You're every corporate cliche about overwork that functions as a lid on whatever's actually happening." A pause. I can hear her breathing. I can hear her apartment in the background — a pot lid clanking, water running. The sounds of a person who exists in one timeline, fully, and thinks that's enough. The unfairness of that thought makes my chest hurt.

There's silence. The particular, loaded brand of it that Mika specializes in — louder than any accusation, more precise than any question. The silence says: I know you're lying, and I'm giving you one more chance to tell the truth.
"You sound like someone who's somewhere else," she says finally. Quiet. Not angry. Worse than angry.
*Halfway across the world*, I think. *Two halfways.*
"Brunch on Saturday," I say. "My treat. That place on Clinton you like."
"Saturday," Mika repeats. She says it like she's holding it up to the light, checking for cracks. "Eleven. Don't flake."
"I won't."
I will. I already know I will, because Saturday is when the ECB is expected to announce a rate decision, and if I can catch it in Timeline B before the New York open on Monday —
"Goodnight, Avery."
"Goodnight."

I hang up. The sticky note on the wall has fully curled now, peeling away from the surface. I press it back down with my thumb and hold it there until the warmth of my skin reactivates the adhesive. There. Fixed.
The wall looks good. The wall looks like control.
I don't call Mika back.
Friday night, and I'm the last one in the office.

It's not intentional. It's just that the quarterly projections won't finish themselves, and every time I think I'm done, another variable presents itself — another number that needs chasing, another assumption that needs testing. The Sterling offices at night are a different geography: the ambient hum of the HVAC system, the cold air that settles like it owns the place after hours, the particular silence of fluorescent lights over empty desks. My fingers move across the keyboard. The radiator clanks once, twice, like a heart that's forgotten its rhythm.
I'm deep in the third projection model when I sense him before I see him. A shift in the air pressure. The specific gravity of Julian Vance entering a space.

I look up. He's in the doorway. No jacket. Sleeves rolled to the elbow, the forearms bare and ropy with the kind of lean muscle that comes from rowing machines and discipline. Tie loosened, knot dropped two inches, top button undone. He looks — human. It's unsettling. Like seeing a cathedral from the inside: all the architecture you admired from the street is actually just scaffolding for something that breathes.
He leans against the doorframe. The wood doesn't creak; it just accepts his weight.
"What's your secret?"
I blink. My fingers have frozen on the keyboard. "Excuse me?"
He says it gently. Almost conversationally. But the words land like stones in still water, each one sending out rings.
"You know things before they happen. You anticipate questions no one's asked yet. You operate like you've already seen the future."

His voice is curious. Not accusatory. But there's an edge beneath the curiosity — the edge of a hunter who's tracked something to its lair and is now standing at the entrance, listening to it breathe. He's been watching. Not monitoring, not supervising — *watching*. The kind of sustained attention that assembles disparate observations into a pattern. The Austrian bank. The commodity pivot. The way I answered a question about Korean semiconductor exports before the data was publicly available.

He's put it together. Not the truth — the truth is insane — but a shape. An outline. He's sketched the silhouette of something impossible and he's waiting for it to move so he can fill in the details.

My heart is slamming against my ribs. The Fork app vibrates in my pocket — not a notification, just a pulse, like a second heartbeat syncing with my own. I feel it through the fabric of my trousers, a tiny insistent drumbeat against my thigh. *Pay attention*, it seems to say. *Something is happening.*

Julian A watches. He's watching now with the same focus he brings to every problem, but warmer. Personal. He sees the microexpression cross my face — whatever flinch or flicker betrays me — and whatever he reads there makes him push off the doorframe.
He walks toward my desk. Slowly. Not threatening. Careful. Like approaching something that might bolt.

The cold air of the office moves with him, or maybe that's just me, my body temperature dropping as my fight-or-flight tries to decide which one this is. He stops at the edge of my desk. Close enough that I can smell him — cedar and something clean, like cold rain on warm stone. Close enough that I can see the fine lines at the corners of his eyes, the ones that only show up when he's not performing authority.

His hand reaches out. I don't breathe. But he's not reaching for me — he's reaching for the quarterly report, the printed copy sitting to my left. He takes it. Skims a page, his eyes moving left to right with the efficiency of someone who processes information the way other people breathe.
Then, still reading, his voice quiet and even: "Whatever you're doing that's making you this good..." He pauses. Turns a page. "...be careful. Brilliance that burns too bright has a short half-life."
He looks up. Meets my eyes. Holds them.

I can't read what's in his gaze — concern, certainly; curiosity, always; and something else, something that might be the same thing I've been catching in fragments all week, the thing that makes the air between us feel like a held breath.

Then he walks out. The quarterly report under his arm. His footsteps recede down the corridor, and the silence he leaves behind has a different quality than the silence that was here before. Thicker. Populated.

I sit at my desk. My heart is a trapped bird. The Fork app is buzzing in my pocket — not stopping, a sustained vibration that feels like it's trying to burrow through the fabric and into my skin. I pull out my phone.
The screen glows.
⚠️ **INTEGRITY: 79%**
⚠️ **NEW: TEMPORAL ECHOES DETECTED**

I stare at the words. Temporal echoes. I don't know what that means. I've read every screen the Fork app has ever shown me, and this phrase has never appeared. It's new. It's a warning about something the app has only now detected, something my daily cheating has catalyzed into existence.
An echo is a repetition. A sound that returns after the original has already moved on. A ghost of a signal bouncing off surfaces it was never meant to touch.

Julian A's words replay in my head: *You operate like you've already seen the future.* He's getting closer. Not to the mechanism — he'd never believe the mechanism — but to the shape of the impossibility. To the fact that something impossible is happening, and it's happening through me.

And now the Fork app is telling me that my impossible actions are creating echoes. Ripples. That the timelines, which I've been treating as separate sandboxes, are starting to talk to each other in ways I can't hear and don't understand.

The phone buzzes again. I think about answering it. I think about closing the app and going home and sleeping — actually sleeping, four consecutive hours in one timeline like a normal person who hasn't found a cheat code that's starting to cheat back.
I don't do any of those things.

I open the app. I read the new warning again. *Temporal echoes detected.* And somewhere underneath the fear — underneath the nosebleeds and the lost time and Mika's silence and Julian A's hunter-gaze — there's a smaller, uglier feeling. A spark.
Because if the timelines are echoing, that means they're not separate anymore. That means information might flow in more than one direction. That means there might be more to cheat.
God help me, I want to know what that means.
I sit in the empty office, the cold air settling around my shoulders like hands, and I start to scroll.
